text.title: Experiment Verdict Template Core Rules
text.summary: The closing-artefact discipline that kills zombie experiments and converts every test into a routed decision plus a captured learning.

rule.statement: Every experiment ends with a verdict card carrying seven fields: id, hypothesis (one sentence from the pre-registration), variant arms, primary metric result (lift, CI, p-value or Bayesian probability), secondary metrics check, verdict (one of five: ship-treatment, ship-control, inconclusive-iterate, inconclusive-stop, harmful-rollback), and the named decision owner.
rule.rationale: Five verdicts cover every honest outcome; teams that allow a "we'll think about it later" sixth state accumulate zombie experiments. The constrained vocabulary forces closure.

rule.statement: Verdicts are written within 5 business days of the experiment's pre-registered close date. Experiments that miss the close date by >5 days are stopped at the platform level (variant assignment paused) until a verdict is written; "running longer for more data" requires an explicit power re-justification recorded as a new pre-registration.
rule.rationale: "Just a bit more data" without re-justification is the classic peeking failure: experiments run until they accidentally cross significance and the team ships a false positive. Forced close + explicit re-justification is the only known cure.

rule.statement: The verdict explicitly addresses pre-registered secondary metrics, not just the primary. Treatment wins on the primary but loses on at least one secondary become "inconclusive-iterate" or "harmful-rollback" by default; overriding to "ship-treatment" requires a named exec sign-off in the card.
rule.rationale: Single-metric verdicts ship treatments that win on conversion but tank retention, refund-rate, or NPS. Forcing secondary review at verdict time catches the tradeoff before rollout.

rule.statement: Each verdict records one "learning" line: a generalisable claim with a confidence tag (weak / moderate / strong) the team can fold back into hypothesis generation. The learning is added to the team's learnings database; verdicts without a learning are bounced back to the experiment owner.
rule.rationale: Without a captured learning, experiments become a Brownian process — the team re-tests the same hypotheses every two quarters. A persistent learning stream raises the bar on what gets queued next.

rule.statement: The verdict routes the next action to a named owner with a target date: ship-treatment → release owner; harmful-rollback → on-call; inconclusive-iterate → experiment owner with the next pre-registration; inconclusive-stop → backlog owner who removes the related backlog items. Cards without a routed action are not closed.
rule.rationale: "Verdict written" without a routed action is the politest form of zombie: the test is closed but nothing happens. Routing-at-close converts verdicts into committed work.

example.code: ## EXP-2026-Q2-031 — Pricing-page hero variant B - hypothesis: shortening the hero copy increases primary CTA clicks for cold traffic - arms: control (long copy) / B (short copy) - primary: CTA CTR — B +6.2% (95% CI [+2.1%, +10.3%], p=0.004) - secondary: signup conversion — B −1.8% (CI [−4.0%, +0.4%], NS); 7-day retention — flat - verdict: ship-treatment with monitoring (CTA win clean, signup non-significant) - learning: shorter hero copy on cold pricing traffic — moderate confidence; revisit when bottom-of-funnel data accrues. - routed: release owner @marina; release ticket REL-1402, target 2026-05-23. Monitoring alert on signup CR.
EXP-2026-Q2-031: B looks better, let's keep it running another week to confirm.
example.note: No close, no verdict vocabulary, no secondary check, no learning, no routed action — classic zombie; the team will rediscover the hypothesis next quarter.
